content.text: An Architecture Decision Record (ADR) is a short document that captures a single significant architectural decision together with its context and consequences. The

is the end-to-end governance process that governs how an ADR moves from an author's first draft through team review and approval, through its active life as policy, and eventually to retirement when the conditions that motivated it no longer apply. Every stage in that journey is assigned a formal status, and transitions between statuses are triggered by defined events and require specific approvals.
In practice, ADRs live alongside the source code they govern, typically in a directory called

) and committed to a git repository. The lifecycle is then enforced through pull-request review, branch protection rules, required approvals, and optional CI automation that lints ADR metadata and validates status transitions before merge.

Agentic AI systems accumulate architectural decisions at a high rate: which LLM to route requests to, how to chunk documents for RAG, what retry policy to apply to tool calls, how to version prompt templates. Without lifecycle governance, these decisions either live only in engineers' heads or become stale documentation that misleads newcomers. A decision recorded as "Accepted" that was quietly reversed six months ago is worse than no record at all — it actively misdirects anyone who reads it.
The lifecycle provides a single source of truth about which decisions are currently in force (

). For teams building on top of LLMs, this matters concretely: a model-selection ADR accepted when GPT-4 was the only capable option may become Deprecated the moment a cheaper open-weights model matches its benchmark scores. Without lifecycle management, the old decision silently outlives its usefulness and continues to shape new work long after the context has changed.

Lifecycle governance also creates a compliance and audit trail. Regulated industries — healthcare, finance, critical infrastructure — increasingly ask teams to demonstrate that AI system decisions were reviewed by qualified stakeholders before deployment. A well-maintained ADR repository with timestamped approval records is a direct answer to that question.
New team members can read the ADR repository to understand not just what decisions were made but why alternatives were rejected. This context is irreplaceable once the original decision-makers leave.
The Proposed → Accepted transition requires approval from a defined quorum, preventing individual engineers from silently committing decisions with broad system impact.
Explicit Deprecated and Superseded statuses surface when a decision is no longer operative, reducing the risk of building new work on obsolete policy.
Machine-readable ADR metadata (YAML front-matter) enables CI linting, PR label automation, and tooling like Log4brains to render searchable decision portals without manual effort.
When two parts of a system reach different conclusions about the same architectural question, the ADR repository is the tiebreaker: what was the accepted decision and who approved it?
Git history combined with ADR status metadata provides a timestamped, auditable record of when decisions were made and by whom — useful for SOC 2, ISO 27001, and AI governance frameworks.

A three-person startup that requires a formal quorum review and two-week SLA for every ADR will find the process slower than the decisions it is documenting. Calibrate review requirements to team size.
ADRs not actively governed accumulate in an Accepted state long after they have been overtaken by events. The lifecycle only provides value if deprecation and supersession are actually triggered.
adr-tools, Log4brains, and custom GitHub Actions do not share a universal schema. Migrating between tools requires transforming frontmatter formats, which can be disruptive.

Long-lived systems accumulate chains of superseded ADRs. Without tooling to follow the chain (e.g., ADR-0012 → ADR-0031 → ADR-0055), it is difficult to know which ADR is currently operative for a given concern.
The lifecycle is a finite state machine with six states. An ADR begins its life as a

: the author has opened a new file, filled in the context and decision sections, and committed it to a feature branch. At this stage the ADR is not yet visible to the full team for formal review — it is a working document.
When the author opens a pull request, the status changes to

. This triggers the review process. The PR description carries the ADR text; reviewers comment inline; the author revises. The review SLA — typically five to ten business days — is the window within which the required approvers must respond. If reviewers request changes, the ADR returns to a revised Draft state on the branch and is resubmitted by force-pushing or adding commits.
If the quorum of required approvers — usually the principal architect plus one domain lead, or the architecture guild — approves the PR, the ADR merges with status
. It is now in force: teams are expected to follow the decision. If reviewers conclusively reject the proposal without a path to revision, the status is set to
and the PR is merged as a record of the rejected proposal (or closed without merging, depending on team convention).
An Accepted ADR remains operative until a defined trigger event occurs.

include model sunset (the provider announces end-of-life for a model version), capability changes (a new model makes a previous capability constraint obsolete), significant cost changes (a 10x price reduction invalidates a cost-optimisation trade-off), or security findings that invalidate the original risk acceptance. When any of these occur, the ADR owner files a PR changing the status to
and records the reason in the
Supersession occurs when a new ADR explicitly replaces an existing one. The new ADR's frontmatter includes a
field. When the new ADR is accepted, the old ADR's status is updated to
link is added, enabling tools to follow the chain to the current operative decision.

attrs.alt: ADR lifecycle state machine: Draft and Proposed states (blue, in-flight) transition to Accepted (green, in-force). Accepted transitions to Deprecated (amber) on sunset or cost change, and to Superseded (purple) when a new ADR replaces it. Proposed can be Rejected (grey, terminal dashed) if review fails quorum.
attrs.title: ADR Lifecycle State Machine

content.text: The diagram shows the six states and their valid transitions.
(blue) represent in-flight work: the ADR is not yet operative.
(green) is the only state in which the ADR is binding policy. Two terminal-or-retirement paths exit Accepted:
(amber) when the decision is no longer valid but no replacement exists, and
(purple) when a newer ADR explicitly takes its place. A Deprecated ADR may also transition to Superseded if a replacement is subsequently filed.
(grey, dashed) is a terminal state for proposals that fail the review quorum.

The full ADR review lifecycle is appropriate whenever a decision will have lasting impact on a system and is difficult to reverse without coordinated effort. For agentic AI systems, this includes model selection, prompt template versioning strategy, chunking strategy for RAG, tool permission scoping, human-in-the-loop thresholds, and data-retention policies for conversation history.
for decisions that cut across more than one service, team, or organisational boundary.
for scoped intra-team decisions where one senior engineer can approve — still valuable for the audit trail, but not worth a full review cycle.
whenever a provider announces end-of-life for a model, an API version is sunset, or a cost structure changes by more than 2x.
Do not use ADRs for implementation details
such as variable naming, code style, or library minor-version pins — these belong in a style guide or package.json, not in a governed decision record.
The following steps establish a governed ADR repository from scratch in a GitHub-hosted project.
directory in the repository. Add a
that defines the ADR format, status vocabulary, and the governance process.
Standardise the YAML front-matter schema for every ADR file:
Install adr-tools or initialise a Log4brains project for rendering. Configure the tool to read from
Write a GitHub Actions workflow (see Examples below) that runs on every PR touching
and lints front-matter required fields, validates status values, and checks that
Configure branch protection on the main branch to require at least two approvals on any PR that modifies the
file to route ADR reviews to the architecture guild.
Define a review SLA in the
: standard ADRs require response within five business days; security or compliance ADRs require response within two business days.
Schedule a quarterly deprecation review: a calendar event that produces a PR scanning all Accepted ADRs for expired contexts, typically triggered by the model provider release cycle.
ADR File with Full YAML Front-matter

---
id: ADR-0042
title: Use claude-3-5-sonnet as default routing target
status: Accepted
date: 2025-03-12
deciders:
  - alice@example.com   # principal architect
  - bob@example.com     # ml-platform lead
categories: [model-selection, cost]
supersedes: ADR-0031
---

## Context

The ML platform routes all non-latency-critical agent requests through a single
default model. ADR-0031 selected gpt-4-turbo-preview as the default in 2024-Q1.
Since then, claude-3-5-sonnet has achieved equivalent scores on our internal eval
suite at 40% lower per-token cost and with a 128k context window.

## Decision

Route all default-tier requests to claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022. Latency-sensitive
paths remain on claude-3-haiku-20240307 per ADR-0038.

## Consequences

**Positive**: 40% cost reduction on default tier. 128k context eliminates most
document-truncation errors. Response quality on internal eval is within 2%.

**Negative**: Teams with GPT-4-specific prompt assumptions must audit prompts.
Migration requires updating 12 prompt templates in the prompt registry.

**Risks**: Model sunset risk: set a calendar reminder to review when Anthropic
announces claude-3-5-sonnet deprecation.

## Review notes

- 2025-03-10: alice approved with minor wording change
- 2025-03-12: bob approved; PR #441 merged
GitHub Actions Workflow for ADR Review Automation

# .github/workflows/adr-governance.yml
# Runs on every PR that touches docs/adr/**
# Enforces: front-matter schema, status vocabulary, supersession integrity

name: ADR Governance

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'docs/adr/**'

jobs:
  lint-adrs:
    name: Lint ADR front-matter
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install pyyaml

      - name: Validate ADR front-matter
        run: |
          python3 - <<'EOF'
          import sys, pathlib, yaml

          VALID_STATUSES = {"Draft", "Proposed", "Accepted",
                            "Deprecated", "Superseded", "Rejected"}
          REQUIRED_FIELDS = {"id", "title", "status", "date", "deciders"}

          adr_dir = pathlib.Path("docs/adr")
          errors = []
          all_ids = set()

          for path in sorted(adr_dir.glob("*.md")):
              text = path.read_text()
              if not text.startswith("---"):
                  errors.append(f"{path}: missing YAML front-matter")
                  continue
              try:
                  fm_raw = text.split("---")[1]
                  fm = yaml.safe_load(fm_raw)
              except Exception as e:
                  errors.append(f"{path}: YAML parse error: {e}")
                  continue

              missing = REQUIRED_FIELDS - set(fm.keys())
              if missing:
                  errors.append(f"{path}: missing fields: {missing}")

              if fm.get("status") not in VALID_STATUSES:
                  errors.append(
                      f"{path}: invalid status '{fm.get('status')}'. "
                      f"Must be one of {VALID_STATUSES}"
                  )

              if fm.get("id"):
                  all_ids.add(fm["id"])

          # Second pass: validate supersession references
          for path in sorted(adr_dir.glob("*.md")):
              text = path.read_text()
              if not text.startswith("---"):
                  continue
              fm = yaml.safe_load(text.split("---")[1])
              for field in ("supersedes", "superseded_by"):
                  ref = fm.get(field)
                  if ref and ref not in all_ids:
                      errors.append(
                          f"{path}: {field} references unknown ADR '{ref}'"
                      )

          if errors:
              for e in errors:
                  print(f"ERROR: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
              sys.exit(1)
          else:
              print(f"OK: validated {len(list(adr_dir.glob('*.md')))} ADRs")
          EOF

  label-pr:
    name: Label ADR PR with status
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    permissions:
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Detect changed ADR statuses
        id: detect
        run: |
          pip install pyyaml -q
          python3 - <<'EOF'
          import subprocess, pathlib, yaml, os

          result = subprocess.run(
              ["git", "diff", "origin/main...HEAD", "--name-only"],
              capture_output=True, text=True
          )
          changed = [f for f in result.stdout.splitlines()
                     if f.startswith("docs/adr/") and f.endswith(".md")]

          labels = set()
          for path in changed:
              text = pathlib.Path(path).read_text()
              if text.startswith("---"):
                  fm = yaml.safe_load(text.split("---")[1])
                  status = fm.get("status", "")
                  labels.add(f"adr:{status.lower()}")

          with open(os.environ["GITHUB_OUTPUT"], "a") as f:
              f.write(f"labels={','.join(labels)}\n")
          EOF

      - name: Apply labels
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const labels = '${{ steps.detect.outputs.labels }}'.split(',').filter(Boolean);
            if (labels.length > 0) {
              await github.rest.issues.addLabels({
                owner: context.repo.owner,
                repo: context.repo.repo,
                issue_number: context.issue.number,
                labels: labels
              });
            }

# .github/workflows/adr-deprecation-check.yml
# Runs quarterly (or on-demand). Opens a GitHub issue listing ADRs
# that may need deprecation review based on age or known triggers.

name: ADR Deprecation Review

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 1 */3 *'  # 09:00 on the 1st of every 3rd month
  workflow_dispatch:        # allow manual trigger

jobs:
  deprecation-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      issues: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Scan for review-eligible ADRs
        id: scan
        run: |
          pip install pyyaml -q
          python3 - <<'EOF'
          import pathlib, yaml, datetime, os

          MAX_AGE_DAYS = 365  # flag ADRs older than 1 year
          today = datetime.date.today()
          candidates = []

          for path in sorted(pathlib.Path("docs/adr").glob("*.md")):
              text = path.read_text()
              if not text.startswith("---"):
                  continue
              fm = yaml.safe_load(text.split("---")[1])
              if fm.get("status") != "Accepted":
                  continue
              adr_date = fm.get("date")
              if isinstance(adr_date, str):
                  adr_date = datetime.date.fromisoformat(adr_date)
              if adr_date and (today - adr_date).days > MAX_AGE_DAYS:
                  candidates.append(
                      f"- **{fm['id']}**: {fm['title']} "
                      f"(accepted {adr_date}, "
                      f"{(today-adr_date).days}d ago)"
                  )

          body = "## Quarterly ADR Deprecation Review\n\n"
          if candidates:
              body += (
                  f"The following {len(candidates)} ADRs are older than "
                  f"{MAX_AGE_DAYS} days and should be reviewed:\n\n"
                  + "\n".join(candidates)
                  + "\n\nFor each: update status to Deprecated or Superseded,"
                  " or add a review comment confirming it remains current."
              )
          else:
              body += "No ADRs require review at this time."

          with open(os.environ["GITHUB_OUTPUT"], "a") as f:
              f.write(f"body<<BODYEOF\n{body}\nBODYEOF\n")
          EOF

      - name: Open review issue
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const body = `${{ steps.scan.outputs.body }}`;
            await github.rest.issues.create({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              title: `Quarterly ADR Deprecation Review — ${new Date().toISOString().slice(0,10)}`,
              body: body,
              labels: ['adr-review', 'architecture']
            });

Consider a mid-sized team building an agentic document-processing system. In early 2024, they accept ADR-0031, which selects GPT-4 Turbo for all summarisation tasks based on a head-to-head eval on their internal test set. The ADR records the context: no competitive alternative existed at similar quality; cost was acceptable at ~$0.01 per document.

In Q3 2025, the team runs its quarterly deprecation workflow. The automated scan flags ADR-0031 as 18 months old. A reviewer notes that claude-3-5-sonnet now scores 3% higher on the same eval at 40% lower cost. The reviewer drafts ADR-0055 with
, opens a PR, and the architecture guild approves it in four days (inside the SLA). When ADR-0055 merges, the CI automation simultaneously updates ADR-0031's status to

. A developer onboarded six months later searches the Log4brains portal for "summarisation model", finds ADR-0031 first (because of its age), immediately sees it is Superseded, and follows the link to ADR-0055 — the current operative decision.

This workflow saved the team from an expensive model migration that would have otherwise required ad-hoc discovery of scattered comments and Slack threads. The cost reduction was enacted with three days of review time and a clean audit trail.

The most common failure mode is not running the deprecation step. Teams that write ADRs diligently but never deprecate them accumulate a repository where most Accepted ADRs describe decisions that were quietly reversed in code. The fix is mandatory — calendar-driven deprecation reviews are not optional hygiene; they are the mechanism that makes the lifecycle trustworthy.
Bypassing the Quorum for Urgent Decisions

During incidents, teams sometimes implement architectural changes without going through the ADR process, intending to backfill the record later. The backfill rarely happens. Define an expedited track in advance: a single principal-architect approval is sufficient for a 24-hour hotfix window, but the ADR must be submitted for full review within five business days or it is automatically moved to Draft.
Accepting a new ADR without updating the old one's status breaks the supersession chain. The ADR linter should enforce that when an ADR's
field is set, the referenced ADR must have
set to the new ADR's ID. Automate this as a post-merge step in the CI workflow to prevent broken links.

to implement it. When an ADR bloats to include implementation details (specific API call signatures, class diagrams), it becomes a living spec that requires updates on every refactor. Keep ADRs concise: context, decision, consequences. Link to implementation docs rather than embedding them.
Do not bundle multiple independent choices into a single ADR. If the choices need separate review or have different lifecycles, file separate ADRs.
An ADR that only records what was decided without explaining what alternatives were rejected (and why) is half-written. Future reviewers will re-litigate the rejected options without this context.

A CONTRIBUTING.md or ADR README should state: "Proposed ADRs require response from all required reviewers within 5 business days. After 10 business days with no response, the ADR author may escalate to the architecture lead."
When a new ADR's PR merges, a post-merge GitHub Action should automatically update the superseded ADR's status field rather than leaving it to the author.
Surface the lifecycle in the developer portal.
Deploy Log4brains as a read-only site accessible to all engineers. Filter by status so teams can quickly identify which ADRs are currently in force versus deprecated.
YAML field listing conditions that should prompt a deprecation review: model sunset, >50% cost change, capability threshold breach.

At small scale (fewer than 50 ADRs), the overhead of a full CI lint run is negligible — the Python validator runs in under two seconds against 50 Markdown files. As the repository grows toward 200–500 ADRs, two concerns emerge: CI time (still trivial) and cognitive load on reviewers (significant).
To manage cognitive load at scale, categorise ADRs with a

front-matter field and configure CODEOWNERS to route each category to the appropriate review group. A model-selection ADR goes to the ML platform guild; a data-retention ADR goes to the security and legal liaisons. This distributes review load rather than concentrating it on a single architecture team.
Log4brains renders all ADRs on a single page by default. At 300+ ADRs, add a
config that groups ADRs by category and enables server-side search.
The quarterly scan is a pure file read — it completes in under 10 seconds even against 1,000 ADRs. No database required.
At organisation scale, consider a dedicated ADR repository that all service repos reference. Use Git submodules or a read-only ADR API endpoint. Supersession links become cross-repo references (
The original Bash-based CLI by Nat Pryce. Commands:
to create a sequentially numbered ADR from a template,
to link a new ADR to an old one and update the old ADR's status. Minimal by design; works via plain Markdown files.

A TypeScript-based ADR management tool that renders a static knowledge-base site from the ADR files. Supports multiple ADR repositories in a monorepo, timeline view, and full-text search. Integrates with GitHub Pages for zero-infrastructure hosting.
The YAML examples in this topic show how to implement ADR linting, PR labelling, and quarterly deprecation reviews directly in GitHub Actions using plain Python and the
action for GitHub API calls. No third-party ADR action from the marketplace is required.

The C4 model tooling includes first-class ADR support: each decision can be attached to an architecture element in the model, enabling traceability from a component to the decisions that shaped its design.
The Backstage developer portal can ingest ADR Markdown files as TechDocs pages, embedding them in a wider service catalog alongside runbooks, API docs, and changelogs.

ADRs with full lifecycle governance sit between plain-file ADRs and heavyweight RFC processes. They deliver strong governance and automation support at lower overhead than RFCs, and complement architecture fitness functions by capturing the rationale that a test cannot express.
The ADR repository is a governance control. Compromising it undermines architectural accountability. Treat it with the same access controls as production infrastructure configuration.

Enable GPG or SSH commit signing with verified signatures required by branch protection. This prevents attribution forgery — without signing, anyone with write access could impersonate an approver in the git log.
Protect the ADR directory with CODEOWNERS.
ensures that even repo admins cannot bypass the required review.
If the CI workflow automatically modifies ADR statuses post-merge (e.g., setting
), ensure the bot commits are attributed to a named service account and that the bot's GitHub token is scoped to minimum permissions (contents: write on the ADR directory only).
Configure the repository to disallow force pushes on the main branch. ADR history is an audit record; rewriting it invalidates the compliance evidence.

ADRs for AI systems may reference internal cost figures, security findings, or competitive analysis. If the repository is public, review each ADR for disclosure risk before merge. Consider a private ADR repository for sensitive decisions with a public-facing summary.
Think of an ADR's lifecycle as analogous to a law's passage. A bill begins as a
when it is introduced for a floor vote, and becomes
(enacted) when it passes with the required majority. Once enacted, it remains operative law until it is repealed (
) or replaced by a newer statute (
). Rejected bills do not become law but are archived to explain what was considered and why it failed.

The analogy holds for the quorum requirement: a bill that sneaks through without the required vote count is illegitimate even if its content is sensible. Likewise, an ADR approved by a single junior engineer without going through the architecture guild has no institutional authority — the quorum is not bureaucracy; it is the source of the ADR's weight in technical debates.

The six ADR lifecycle states (Draft, Proposed, Accepted, Deprecated, Superseded, Rejected) form a finite state machine. Only Accepted ADRs are in-force policy; the others are either in-flight or retired.

The Proposed → Accepted transition requires a defined quorum (typically principal architect + domain lead) within a stated review SLA. This is the governance gate that gives ADRs institutional authority.

Deprecation triggers for agentic AI ADRs are domain-specific: model sunset, capability threshold changes, and significant cost structure shifts are the most common. Record them explicitly in the ADR's
Supersession must link in both directions: the new ADR's
field. Automate this link as a post-merge CI step to prevent broken chains.
A YAML front-matter linter in CI enforces required fields, validates status vocabulary, and checks supersession reference integrity — preventing malformed ADRs from reaching the main branch.

Scheduled quarterly deprecation scans are not optional hygiene — they are the mechanism that keeps the Accepted ADR pool trustworthy. Without them, Accepted accumulates stale decisions that actively mislead.
Log4brains renders a searchable, status-filtered portal from plain Markdown ADR files, making the lifecycle visible to the whole engineering organisation without manual publishing effort.
